title: Storytelling Reference Bank
subtitle: A curated toolkit for writers at every stage

categories.label: Foundation
categories.description: Principles of craft drawn from writers across millennia and traditions. Engage critically — including with whose voices are centered.

items.title: The Craft of Lasting Work
items.hook: From Plato to Toni Morrison — what separates forgettable writing from work that outlives its author. The canon here skews Western and European; read it with that in mind alongside the global frameworks.

items.title: Master Techniques
items.hook: The Iceberg, The Slant, The Paradox, The Voice. Fourteen practical tools with diagnostic questions and worked examples.

categories.label: Story Architecture
categories.description: Frameworks from multiple traditions — Western, Japanese, South Asian, West African. Each solves a different structural problem. None is the default.

items.title: Three-Act Structure
items.hook: A dominant Western framework — 25% Setup, 50% Confrontation, 25% Resolution — with precise beat positions and common failure modes.

items.title: Save the Cat — 15 Beats
items.hook: Blake Snyder's beat-by-beat map. More granular than Three-Act — use it for precision planning and diagnosing structural sag.

items.title: Scene-Sequel Pacing
items.hook: Micro-structure for individual scenes. Controls reader breathing room and keeps momentum from collapsing.

items.title: Kishotenketsu
items.hook: A Japanese four-act structure that creates revelation without conflict. Not an alternative to Three-Act — a different premise entirely about how meaning forms.

items.title: Global Frameworks
items.hook: Rasa (Indian aesthetic theory, ~200 BCE), Jo-ha-kyū (Japanese temporal rhythm, ~1400 CE), Griot (West African oral tradition, ~13th century). Complete, sophisticated frameworks in their own right — not supplements to anything.

categories.label: Character & World
categories.description: Tools for building people worth caring about and worlds worth inhabiting.

items.title: Character Development Sheet
items.hook: From core wound to behavioral tic — a working template for building characters who feel true rather than designed. Reading time: 2 min. Working time: 20–30 min per character.

items.title: World-Building Framework
items.hook: From physical geography to economy to myth — a working template for worlds that feel lived-in. Reading time: 4 min. Working time: 45+ min if you go deep.

categories.label: Genre
categories.description: What each genre promises readers — and what it demands from writers. Know the rules before you break them.

items.title: Genre Guide
items.hook: Conventions, reader expectations, and subversion strategies for every major genre — literary, thriller, romance, fantasy, SF, mystery, horror, memoir, non-fiction.

items.title: Children's Picture Book
items.hook: The 600-word, 32-page constraint. More demanding than it looks — every word is a commitment, every page a turn.

categories.label: Revision
categories.description: How to see your own work clearly and make it better — without losing what's alive in it.

items.title: Revision Workflow
items.hook: Phased approach: structural first, then scene, then line. Don't polish before you've cut. Don't cut before you understand the shape.

categories.label: Science Stories
categories.description: How to write scientist characters that feel real — using NGSS science practices as dramatic engines, not classroom props.

items.title: Scientist Story Planning Guide
items.hook: How to make a student feel science from the inside — the uncertainty, the obsession, the fights with colleagues, the moment the world reorganizes itself.

items.title: Scientist Story Template
items.hook: Fill-in planning framework for a scientist narrative aligned to NGSS science practices and Missouri Learning Standards.

items.title: Cross-Curricular Story Matrix
items.hook: Story planning that weaves science, history, ELA, and math standards into a single narrative. Worked examples included.
